chapterSummary.title: Sharing and Measuring
chapterSummary.content: This chapter introduces division as the process of equal sharing and grouping. Students learn to divide objects, lengths, weights, and quantities fairly among people or into equal groups. The chapter builds a strong connection between multiplication and division, showing that every multiplication fact gives two division facts. Students also learn about remainders — what is left over when things cannot be shared perfectly. Through real-life problems like sharing food, cutting ribbon, packing items, and distributing things, students see how division is used every day.
keyPoints: Division means splitting a total into equal groups or sharing equally
The parts of division are: dividend ÷ divisor = quotient (with possible remainder)
Every multiplication fact gives two division facts (e.g., 5 x 6 = 30 gives 30 ÷ 5 = 6 and 30 ÷ 6 = 5)
A remainder is what is left over when things cannot be divided equally
You can check division by multiplying the quotient by the divisor
Special rules: n ÷ 1 = n, n ÷ n = 1, 0 ÷ n = 0
learningObjectives: Understand division as equal sharing and grouping
Know the terms dividend, divisor, quotient, and remainder
Use multiplication facts to solve division problems
Solve division word problems involving measurement and sharing

curatedNotes.title: Fun Activity: Share the Sweets
curatedNotes.content: Take 24 small objects (like coins, buttons, or stones). Try sharing them equally among 2, 3, 4, 6, and 8 groups. Write the division sentence for each one. For example, 24 ÷ 2 = 12, 24 ÷ 3 = 8, and so on. Now try 24 ÷ 5. You will find a remainder! How much is left over?

curatedNotes.title: Quick Trick: Use Multiplication Tables Backwards
curatedNotes.content: Division becomes super easy when you know your multiplication tables! For any division like 48 ÷ 6, just think: 'What number times 6 gives 48?' Look through the 6 times table: 6 x 8 = 48. So 48 ÷ 6 = 8! This trick works for all division facts. The better you know your tables, the faster you can divide.

curatedNotes.title: Sharing vs Grouping: Two Ways to Think About Division
curatedNotes.content: Division can tell two different stories. Sharing: 'I have 20 toffees and 4 friends. How many does each friend get?' (20 ÷ 4 = 5 each). Grouping: 'I have 20 toffees and want to make packets of 5. How many packets can I make?' (20 ÷ 5 = 4 packets). Both use division but ask different questions. Practice spotting which type each word problem is!
